summary: Every invoice can change an exact product total, every product must appear, and arbitrary unique names must be ordered; the left join, grouped aggregation, and final sort match the resulting input and comparison-order lower bound.

optimality.argument: Inspecting all I invoices is necessary because any amount can change a required total, while emitting P products in ascending order by arbitrary unique names has an Omega(P log P) comparison lower bound without a name index. Hash joining and aggregation take O(I + P) expected work, and sorting the P groups takes O(P log P).

replacement_checks.evidence: Any invoice amount may alter one output aggregate, and arbitrary unique product names require comparison ordering of all P result rows.
A hash left join plus grouped sums processes each input row once, retains P group states, and a final comparison sort orders those groups.

Authored fixtures cover the public sample, products without invoices, zero-valued invoices, mixed empty and invoiced products, independent amount columns, identifier gaps, row-order independence, and required name ordering.
